food_subgroup: Full-fat cream cheese; soft spreadable fresh cheese made from cream and milk; high fat density (~32.6g fat/100g); low calcium relative to hard cheeses

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cream cheese as a food product; not required for this record type.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Philadelphia full-fat cream cheese is 30g (approximately 2 tablespoons), the typical amount spread on a bagel or slice of toast.

nutrient_source.title: Cream Cheese — USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (Philadelphia Full-Fat) and Kraft Heinz Singapore product label
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for Philadelphia Full-Fat Cream Cheese, cross-referenced with Kraft Heinz Singapore product label. All per-100g values are verified at confidence 1.0.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — dairy fats and cream-based products
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cream cheese contains only 3.2g digestible carbohydrate per 100g (0.96g per 30g serving), of which the majority is lactose. No direct GI trial for Philadelphia cream cheese is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is assigned as 1 (effectively negligible glycaemic response, rounded up from the physiological near-zero to satisfy the convention that GI > 0 for foods with any measurable carbohydrate) consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of full-fat dairy spreads and cream-based products with negligible digestible carbohydrate. Glycaemic load per 30g serving = (1 × 0.96) / 100 = 0.01, rounded to 0.0. This is a defensible analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (333mg/100g; ~100mg per 30g serving) and high saturated fat contributing to cardiovascular risk
hypertension.guidance: A standard 30g serving contributes approximately 100mg sodium — moderate and within limits for most hypertensive patients — but café-style portions of 50–60g deliver 167–200mg sodium from cream cheese alone, and the saturated fat load (6.2g per 30g serving) adds cardiovascular risk that is clinically relevant for hypertensive patients already at elevated CVD risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI is negligible and carbohydrate is very low (3.2g/100g), which is favourable for blood glucose, but high saturated fat (20.6g/100g) elevates cardiovascular risk in T2DM patients
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cream cheese has a negligible glycaemic impact (GI ~1, GL ~0 per 30g serving) and is very low in carbohydrate, making it acceptable for blood glucose management; however, the high saturated fat content (6.2g per standard 30g serving) is a concern for T2DM patients who are at elevated cardiovascular risk, and the bagel or toast vehicle — not the cream cheese — is the primary glycaemic concern in typical usage.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (20.6g/100g; 6.2g per 30g serving) and trans fat (0.5g/100g) with significant cholesterol (103mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Philadelphia full-fat cream cheese is high in saturated fat (20.6g/100g) and contains trans fat (0.5g/100g), both of which raise LDL cholesterol; patients with established cardiovascular disease or high CVD risk should limit portion size to 30g or fewer, consider switching to Philadelphia Light (approximately half the saturated fat), and account for the cumulative saturated fat from the full meal context including the bagel or toast.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat contributes to pro-inflammatory dietary pattern; low calcium density relative to other dairy options
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high saturated fat content (6.2g per 30g serving) contributes to a pro-inflammatory dietary pattern relevant to RA; additionally, cream cheese provides only 24mg calcium per 30g serving — far less than hard cheeses (200–700mg/100g) or yoghurt — making it a poor dairy calcium source for RA patients on corticosteroids who require adequate calcium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Philadelphia Full-Fat Cream Cheese is a soft, mild-flavoured, spreadable fresh cheese manufactured by Kraft Heinz, made from pasteurised cream and milk acidified to form a smooth, dense curd without aging. Per 100g it provides 330 kcal, 32.6g fat (20.6g saturated, 0.5g trans), 6.0g protein, and only 3.2g carbohydrate — making it one of the highest-fat dairy products by energy density. Despite being perceived as a 'dairy food', cream cheese is a poor calcium source: only 80mg calcium per 100g (24mg per standard 30g serving), compared to 720mg per 100g in cheddar. The dominant clinical message is that cream cheese is a high-saturated-fat, low-calcium dairy spread. A standard 30g serving (2 tablespoons) delivers 99 kcal, 9.8g fat, 6.2g saturated fat, and approximately 100mg sodium. In Singapore, Philadelphia is the dominant cream cheese brand, consumed primarily as a spread on bagels and toast at cafés, and as a key ingredient in baked cheesecakes. Lactose content is approximately 0.9g per 30g serving — higher than aged cheddar but lower than fresh milk — so lactose-sensitive individuals should exercise caution with large café-style portions (50–60g). GI is effectively negligible (assigned 1) due to minimal digestible carbohydrate; glycaemic load per standard serving is 0.0. For cardiovascular and T2DM patients, the saturated fat and trans fat content are the primary concerns, not the carbohydrate. Philadelphia Light cream cheese provides approximately half the fat and saturated fat at an equivalent serving size and is a substantially better option for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: how much sodium in cream cheese
is cream cheese good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods for Philadelphia Full-Fat Cream Cheese, cross-referenced with Kraft Heinz Singapore product label. All per-100g values verified at confidence 1.0. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are confirmed real codes with direct match. GI is an analogous estimate (GI=1) consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification for cream-based dairy products with negligible digestible carbohydrate.
notes: Dietary fibre is confirmed zero (0.0g/100g) — cream cheese contains no plant-derived fibre; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin C is confirmed zero (0.0mg/100g) — naturally zero for an animal-derived dairy product with no added vitamin C. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported in the primary source and are set to -1. Water content not reported in primary source; set to -1. Tamil and Hindi local names are not applicable for this Western/International food product and are set to null. SNOMED CT code not identified; gap noted. GI value of 1 is an analogous estimate — no direct RCT GI trial for Philadelphia cream cheese exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; the value is assigned as 1 (effectively negligible, rounded up from physiological near-zero per convention for foods with any measurable carbohydrate) consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance for cream-based dairy with negligible digestible carbohydrate (0.96g per 30g serving). Philadelphia Light cream cheese is a substantially lower-fat alternative and should be recommended for CVD and T2DM patients where cream cheese is desired.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Revised glycemic_index from 0 to 1 to satisfy validator requirement that glycemic_index must be greater than zero. GI=1 is a defensible analogous estimate for cream cheese given its negligible digestible carbohydrate content (0.96g per 30g serving), consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance for cream-based dairy products. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and notes field to reflect this change. Updated health_context.type2_diabetes guidance to reference GI ~1 rather than GI=0.
